title: Regulatory Incident Response Time Trending
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel/competent-authority review.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.9.4` result set, dashboard panel, and archived audit evidence export for Regulatory Incident Response Time Trending.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.33 requires breach notification to the supervisory authority 'without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours'. Trending regulatory incident response times evidences that the organisation measures and is meeting that obligation over time.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.33 (Breach notification to supervisory authority) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.9.4: Regulatory Incident Response Time Trending.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.9.4' running on notable — filter with tag/category/rule_name for compliance/regulatory work; fields closed_time, _time, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.23 mandates phased incident reporting (24h early warning, 72h notification, 1-month final report). Trending MTTR and MTTF (mean time to first report) against these windows is the operational evidence of compliance.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.23 (Reporting obligations) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.9.4: Regulatory Incident Response Time Trending.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.9.4' running on notable — filter with tag/category/rule_name for compliance/regulatory work; fields closed_time, _time, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A representative NIS2 evidence gap for Regulatory Incident Response Time Trending appears in the search results with the responsible owner and affected asset/case.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A current, approved, in-scope record with complete evidence does not appear in the exception results.

dataSources: `` `notable` `` — filter with `tag`/`category`/`rule_name` for compliance/regulatory work; fields `closed_time`, `_time`
spl:
`notable` earliest=-730d (tag="compliance" OR category="*compliance*" OR rule_name="*regulatory*" OR like(rule_name,"%compliance%"))
| eval mttr_sec=if(isnotnull(closed_time) AND closed_time>_time, closed_time-_time, null())
| where isnotnull(mttr_sec)
| timechart span=90d avg(mttr_sec) as avg_mttr_sec perc95(mttr_sec) as p95_mttr_sec
| eval avg_mttr_h=avg_mttr_sec/3600
| trendline sma2(avg_mttr_sec) as mttr_trend
| streamstats window=2 global=f first(avg_mttr_sec) as prev_q_mttr
| eval vs_prev_q_pct=if(isnotnull(prev_q_mttr) AND prev_q_mttr>0,round(100*(avg_mttr_sec-prev_q_mttr)/prev_q_mttr,1),null())
| predict avg_mttr_sec as mttr_forecast algorithm=LLP future_timespan=2 period=4
description: Mean time to resolve compliance-tagged incidents by quarter proves that regulatory and policy breaches are handled with discipline—supporting supervisory expectations and internal KPIs beyond generic IT MTTR.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR, NIS2 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Define a consistent ES tag or naming convention for regulatory notables; (2) confirm `closed_time` population for closed incidents (`| fieldsummary closed_time`); (3) exclude false positives with a lookup of excluded `rule_name` values; (4) compare quarterly MTTR to IT-wide MTTR in a separate panel for context; (5) document scope (which jurisdictions or policies) in the dashboard subtitle.
visualization: Line chart (`avg_mttr_h` or `avg_mttr_sec`, `mttr_trend`, `mttr_forecast`), column chart (`p95_mttr_sec` by quarter), single value (`vs_prev_q_pct`).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Assessment cycle alignment** — Control assessments following annual cycles may show brief non-current periods between assessment completion and report publication.

2. **Finding remediation within SLA** — Open findings within their documented remediation timeline are being addressed per policy, not neglected or overlooked.

3. **Framework version transitions** — Migration between control framework versions (ISO 27001:2013 to 2022) creates temporary mapping gaps during the documented transition period.

4. **Accepted residual risk items** — Findings formally accepted through the risk management governance process with documented compensating controls and management sign-off.

5. **Evidence collection cadence differences** — Controls with different evidence generation frequencies (real-time vs. quarterly) show varying currency in compliance dashboards.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.33`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.33 (Breach notification to supervisory authority) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.9.4: Regulatory Incident Response Time Trending." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Define a consistent ES tag or naming convention for regulatory notables. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** confirm `closed_time` population for closed incidents (`| fieldsummary closed_time`). This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** exclude false positives with a lookup of excluded `rule_name` values. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 4:** compare quarterly MTTR to IT-wide MTTR in a separate panel for context. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** document scope (which jurisdictions or policies) in the dashboard subtitle. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

`streamstats` computes a running aggregate over events in sequence, preserving event order. For "Regulatory Incident Response Time Trending" specifically, this is the right choice because the control asks a temporal question — detecting accelerating trends, windowed thresholds, or deviations from a rolling baseline.

For "Regulatory Incident Response Time Trending," the aggregation exposes temporal structure that a single point-in-time reading cannot — the compliance question is about behaviour over a period, not at an instant.

The grouping `by the grouping fields` is the analytical unit — the aggregate is computed within each distinct combination. A common mistake is grouping by a field that has cardinality near 1:1 with events, which produces a group-per-event and makes the window stat trivially equal to the event value.

Order sensitivity: `streamstats` respects the event ordering presented to it, which means the preceding `sort` (or the natural _time-descending default of the base search) dictates behaviour. If the stream is unsorted or sorted the wrong way, the running totals are meaningless. Always precede `streamstats` with `| sort 0 _time` to guarantee chronological order.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.9.4: Regulatory Incident Response Time Trending]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -730d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.9.4,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `avg_mttr_h`, `mttr_sec`, `vs_prev_q_pct`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table avg_mttr_h, mttr_sec, vs_prev_q_pct
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `prev_q_mttr`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(prev_q_mttr) as p50 perc90(prev_q_mttr) as p90 perc99(prev_q_mttr) as p99 max(prev_q_mttr) as hi
```

The current threshold `0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.9.4: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Line Chart (``avg_mttr_h` or `avg_mttr_sec``, ``mttr_trend``, ``mttr_forecast``):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Distribution (``p95_mttr_sec` by quarter`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (``vs_prev_q_pct``):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Regulatory Incident Response Time Trending. We watch trends so leaders see improvement, not a one-time snapshot.
